• Declining cash balances
• Stretching payables
• Drawing on credit lines
• Deteriorating ratios

sections.title: Liquidity Risk Mitigation
sections.content:
**Cash Flow Forecasting:**
• Rolling 13-week forecast
• Multiple scenarios
• Stress testing

**Diversify Funding:**
• Multiple banks
• Different instruments
• Stagger maturities
• Mix fixed/floating

**Working Capital Efficiency:**
• Accelerate collections
• Optimize inventory
• Manage payables wisely

**Contingency Planning:**
• Identify backup sources
• Maintain relationships
• Pre-arrange facilities
• Asset pledging options

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Liquidity vs Solvency
sections.content:
**Don't confuse these!**

**Liquidity:**
• Short-term focus
• Can pay current obligations?
• Current ratio, quick ratio
• Operating cash flow

**Solvency:**
• Long-term focus
• Total assets > Total liabilities?
• Debt-to-equity ratio
• Interest coverage

**Key Point:**
A company can be SOLVENT (assets > liabilities)
but ILLIQUID (can't pay tomorrow's obligations)

This is why cash flow matters - timing matters!

Profitable companies fail when they run out of cash!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Liquidity risk: Unable to meet short-term obligations when due
Two types: Funding liquidity and market liquidity
Key ratios: Current, quick, cash ratio, CCC
Sources: Internal (operations, assets) and external (credit)
Warning signs: Declining cash, stretched payables, credit draws
Mitigation: Forecasting, diversified funding, WC efficiency
Liquidity ≠ Solvency: Profitable firms can be illiquid

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has: Cash $50K, Receivables $150K, Inventory $200K, Current Liabilities $250K. What is the quick ratio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Quick ratio = (Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities = ($50K + $150K) / $250K = $200K / $250K = 0.80. This excludes inventory because it's less liquid than cash or receivables.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Quick ratio excludes inventory. Use (Cash + Receivables) / Current Liabilities.

title: Hedging Strategies
description: Use derivatives and other tools to manage financial risks

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Hedging protects the company from adverse price movements! CMAs evaluate hedging strategies, account for derivatives, and measure hedge effectiveness. Understanding hedging helps manage currency, interest rate, and commodity price risks.

sections.title: What is Hedging?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Reducing risk by taking an offsetting position

**Purpose:**
• Reduce earnings volatility
• Protect against adverse moves
• Make outcomes more predictable
• NOT speculation for profit

**What Can Be Hedged:**
• Currency exchange rates
• Interest rates
• Commodity prices
• Equity prices

**Trade-off:**
• Protection comes at a cost
• Limit downside BUT also upside
• Basis risk remains

sections.title: Derivative Instruments
rows: Agreement to buy/sell at future date

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Forwards vs Options
sections.content:
**Forward/Future:**
• OBLIGATION to transact
• No upfront cost (futures have margin)
• Lock in price - no flexibility
• Symmetric payoff (gains/losses)

**Option:**
• RIGHT, not obligation
• Pay premium upfront
• Flexibility - only use if beneficial
• Asymmetric payoff (limited loss, unlimited gain)

**When to Use:**
• Forward: High confidence in exposure
• Option: Want protection but keep upside

**Example:**
US company due to receive €1M in 90 days
• Forward: Lock in $/€ rate now
• Option: Pay premium for right to sell € at set rate

sections.title: Currency Hedging
sections.content:
**Transaction Exposure:**
• Known future receipts/payments
• Hedge with forwards, options

**Translation Exposure:**
• Converting subsidiary financials
• Balance sheet effect
• Less commonly hedged

**Economic Exposure:**
• Long-term competitive position
• Operational hedges (diversify)

**Natural Hedging:**
• Match currency of revenues and costs
• Borrow in same currency as assets
• No derivatives needed

**Leading and Lagging:**
• Accelerate or delay payment timing
• Based on currency expectations

sections.title: Interest Rate Hedging
sections.content:
**Risk:**
Floating rate debt → Payment uncertainty

**Interest Rate Swap:**
• Exchange floating for fixed payments
• Counterparty exchanges fixed for floating
• Net settlement periodically

**Example:**
• Company has $10M floating debt (SOFR + 2%)
• Enter swap: Receive floating, pay 5% fixed
• Net effect: Fixed 5% + 2% = 7% locked

**Caps and Floors:**
• Cap: Maximum rate (buy call)
• Floor: Minimum rate (buy put)
• Collar: Buy cap, sell floor (reduces cost)

**Fixed for Floating:**
Sometimes companies want floating exposure!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Hedge Accounting
sections.content:
**Accounting for Hedges (ASC 815):**

**Without Hedge Accounting:**
• Derivative: Mark to market through P&L
• Hedged item: Different timing
• Creates volatility (mismatch)

**With Hedge Accounting:**
• Match timing of gains/losses
• Reduces artificial volatility

**Types:**
• **Fair Value Hedge:** Offset changes in asset/liability
• **Cash Flow Hedge:** Hedge future cash flows, gains to OCI
• **Net Investment Hedge:** Foreign subsidiary investment

**Requirements:**
• Formal documentation
• Effectiveness testing
• Strict rules - frequently tested!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Hedging: Reduce risk through offsetting positions
Derivatives: Forwards, futures, options, swaps
Forward: Obligation, no upfront cost, locks in price
Option: Right (not obligation), pay premium, keeps upside
Currency hedging: Transaction, translation, economic exposure
Interest rate swap: Exchange floating for fixed payments
Hedge accounting: Matches timing, reduces P&L volatility

knowledgeCheck.question: A company wants to protect against currency depreciation but still benefit if the currency appreciates. Which instrument is most appropriate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Options provide the right but not obligation to transact, allowing the company to benefit from favorable currency movements while protecting against adverse ones. Forwards lock in a rate regardless of subsequent movements, eliminating both downside and upside.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which instrument provides downside protection while preserving upside?

title: Insurance and Risk Transfer
description: Use insurance and other mechanisms to transfer risks

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Insurance is a key risk management tool! CMAs evaluate insurance decisions, manage self-insurance reserves, and analyze the cost-benefit of risk transfer. Understanding insurance options helps optimize the risk management mix.

sections.title: Risk Response Options
sections.content:
**Four Risk Treatment Options:**

**1. Avoid:**
• Don't take the risk
• Exit activity

**2. Reduce/Mitigate:**
• Controls to lower likelihood/impact
• Prevention and detection

**3. Transfer:**
• Insurance
• Contracts
• Outsourcing
• Hedging

**4. Accept/Retain:**
• Accept the residual risk
• Self-insure
• Budget for losses

**Selection:**
Based on likelihood, impact, and cost

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Insurance Decisions
sections.content:
**When to Buy Insurance:**

**Insure When:**
• Potential loss is catastrophic
• Can't absorb loss
• Required by contract/regulation
• Cost is reasonable vs risk

**Self-Insure (Retain) When:**
• Losses are predictable
• Can absorb financially
• Insurance is expensive
• Better risk information than insurer

**Key Analysis:**
• Expected losses
• Insurance premium
• Administrative costs
• Cash flow impact
• Risk tolerance

sections.title: Self-Insurance
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Retaining risk rather than transferring to insurer

**How It Works:**
• Set aside reserves for expected losses
• Pay claims directly
• May buy excess coverage for large losses

**Advantages:**
• Avoid insurer profit margin
• Cash flow benefits
• Control over claims
• Incentive to reduce losses

**Disadvantages:**
• Volatile costs
• Large loss exposure
• Administrative burden

**Hybrid Approaches:**
• High deductibles
• Self-insured retention (SIR)
• Captive insurance companies

sections.title: Captive Insurance
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Company-owned insurer for own risks

**How It Works:**
• Form subsidiary insurance company
• Pay premiums to captive
• Captive pays claims
• Often in favorable jurisdiction

**Advantages:**
• Access reinsurance markets
• Retain underwriting profit
• Tax deductible premiums
• Cover hard-to-insure risks
• Stabilize costs

**Disadvantages:**
• Capital requirements
• Regulatory compliance
• Still retain ultimate risk

**Common Uses:**
• Large companies
• Healthcare organizations

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Insurance Concepts
sections.content:
**Know These Terms:**

**Premium:**
• Amount paid for coverage
• Usually annual

**Deductible:**
• Amount insured pays first
• Higher deductible = lower premium

**Policy Limit:**
• Maximum insurer pays
• Per occurrence or aggregate

**Coinsurance:**
• Sharing of cost after deductible
• 80/20: Insurer pays 80%

**Indemnification:**
• Restore to pre-loss position
• Can't profit from insurance

**Subrogation:**
• Insurer's right to pursue third party

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Risk responses: Avoid, reduce, transfer, accept
Insurance types: Property, liability, D&O, business interruption, cyber
Insure catastrophic, unpredictable risks
Self-insure predictable, manageable losses
Self-insurance: Reserves, direct payment, volatile
Captive insurance: Company-owned insurer for own risks
Key terms: Premium, deductible, limit, coinsurance, subrogation

knowledgeCheck.question: A company forms a wholly-owned subsidiary to insure its own risks and access reinsurance markets. This arrangement is called:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A captive insurance company is a wholly-owned subsidiary formed to insure the parent's risks. It allows the company to retain underwriting profits, access reinsurance, and potentially deduct premiums while still ultimately retaining the risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which arrangement involves a company-owned insurer?

title: Currency Risk Management
description: Manage foreign exchange exposure

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Global operations mean currency risk! CMAs must understand how exchange rate movements affect cash flows, financial statements, and competitive position. Currency risk management protects profitability and cash flows from adverse FX movements. Essential for any company with international operations.

sections.title: Types of Currency Exposure
sections.content:
**Transaction Exposure:**
• Impact on cash flows
• From existing obligations
• A/R, A/P in foreign currency
• Most directly managed

**Translation Exposure:**
• Impact on financial statements
• Converting subsidiary results
• Accounting, not economic
• Often not hedged

**Economic/Operating Exposure:**
• Impact on competitive position
• Future cash flows
• Long-term strategic
• Hardest to measure

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Hedging Instruments
sections.content:
**Forward Contract:**
• Agreement to exchange at future date
• Locked-in rate
• Obligation (must execute)
• Customized, OTC

**Futures Contract:**
• Similar to forward
• Exchange-traded, standardized
• Daily settlement
• More liquid

**Currency Option:**
• Right, not obligation
• Pay premium
• Protects downside
• Keeps upside potential

**Money Market Hedge:**
• Borrow/lend to offset
• Match currency of asset/liability
• Natural hedge

sections.title: Transaction Exposure Example
sections.content:
**Scenario:**
US company to receive €1,000,000 in 90 days
Current spot: $1.10/€
90-day forward: $1.08/€

**Unhedged:**
• If € strengthens to $1.15: Receive $1,150,000
• If € weakens to $1.05: Receive $1,050,000
• Range: $100,000 depending on rates

**Forward Hedge:**
• Sell €1M forward at $1.08
• Guaranteed: $1,080,000
• No upside, no downside

**Option Hedge:**
• Buy put option at $1.10 strike
• Pay premium (say $15,000)
• If € < $1.10: Exercise, get $1.10
• If € > $1.10: Let expire, get spot rate

sections.title: Natural Hedging
sections.content:
**Operational Strategies:**
• Match revenues and costs in same currency
• Source from countries where you sell
• Borrow in currency of revenues
• Invoice in home currency

**Leading and Lagging:**
• **Lead:** Accelerate payment if currency weakening
• **Lag:** Delay payment if currency strengthening

**Netting:**
• Offset intra-company payables/receivables
• Only settle net amount
• Reduces transaction costs

**Benefits:**
• No derivative costs
• Reduces but doesn't eliminate exposure
• Strategic, long-term approach

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Forwards vs Options
sections.content:
**Forward Contracts:**
• OBLIGATION to transact
• Locks in rate exactly
• No upside if rates move favorably
• No premium cost
• Best when: Want certainty

**Options:**
• RIGHT, not obligation
• Protection with participation
• Keep upside potential
• Require premium payment
• Best when: Want protection + upside

**Common Exam Distinction:**
• 'Must exercise' = Forward/Futures
• 'Can choose' = Option
• Option cost = Premium

**Hedging Policy:**
Companies often hedge transaction exposure
Often choose NOT to hedge translation exposure

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three types: Transaction, Translation, Economic exposure
Transaction: Known, affects actual cash flows
Translation: Accounting, affects reported numbers
Hedging tools: Forwards, futures, options, money market
Forwards: Lock in rate, obligation
Options: Pay premium, keep upside, right not obligation
Natural hedges: Match currencies, netting, leading/lagging

knowledgeCheck.question: A US company will pay £10 million in 60 days. To hedge this exposure, the company should:
options: Do nothing since translation exposure is unhedgeable
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The company has a pound payable (transaction exposure). To hedge, it should buy pounds forward to lock in the exchange rate. This ensures certainty about the dollar cost of the payment regardless of exchange rate movements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Match the hedge to the exposure - payable means you need to BUY the currency.

title: Credit Risk Management
description: Assess and manage customer credit risk

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Bad debts directly reduce profitability! CMAs support credit decisions that balance sales growth with collection risk. Effective credit management protects cash flow while enabling competitive customer terms. This overlaps with working capital management.

sections.title: Credit Risk Overview
sections.content:
**What is Credit Risk?**
• Risk that customer won't pay
• Results in bad debt expense
• Affects cash flow and profitability

**Trade Credit:**
• Company extends credit to customers
• Creates accounts receivable
• Balance: Sales vs bad debts

**Credit Policy Elements:**
• Credit standards (who qualifies)
• Credit terms (payment period)
• Collection procedures

**Goal:**
Maximize profitable sales while minimizing bad debts

sections.title: The Five Cs of Credit
rows: Ability to pay from cash flow

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Credit Decision Analysis
sections.content:
**Should We Extend Credit?**

**Expected Profit from Credit Sale:**
= (1 - Bad debt %) × Contribution margin
- (Bad debt % × Variable cost)

**Simplified:**
If Contribution margin > Expected bad debt loss
→ Extend credit

**Example:**
• Selling price: $100
• Variable cost: $60
• CM: $40
• Bad debt probability: 10%

**Expected value:**
0.90 × $40 - 0.10 × $60
= $36 - $6 = $30 expected profit
→ Extend credit!

**Break-even bad debt rate:**
CM / Sales price = $40 / $100 = 40%

sections.title: Credit Terms and Discounts
sections.content:
**Standard Terms Format:**
2/10, n/30
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Full amount due in 30 days

**Setting Credit Terms:**
• Industry norms
• Competitive necessity
• Customer bargaining power
• Cost of carrying receivables

**Loosening Credit Standards:**
• Increases sales
• Increases bad debts
• Increases carrying costs
• Net profit impact depends on margins

**Tightening Credit Standards:**
• Opposite effects
• Reduces risk but may lose sales

sections.title: Credit Analysis Tools
sections.content:
**Information Sources:**
• Credit bureaus (D&B, Experian)
• Financial statements
• Bank references
• Trade references
• Payment history

**Key Ratios:**
• Current ratio, quick ratio
• Debt-to-equity
• Times interest earned
• Days sales outstanding

**Credit Scoring:**
• Quantitative models
• Weight multiple factors
• Produce credit score
• Automate decisions

**Monitoring:**
• Age accounts receivable
• Track payment patterns
• Watch for warning signs

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Changing Credit Policy
sections.content:
**Incremental Analysis:**

When evaluating credit policy changes:

**Loosening Standards - Consider:**
+ Additional contribution margin
- Additional bad debt losses
- Additional carrying costs
= Net change in profit

**Example:**
• New sales: $500,000
• CM ratio: 30%
• Additional CM: $150,000
• Bad debt rate: 8%: $40,000
• Carrying cost: $10,000
• Net benefit: $100,000

**Common Mistake:**
Forgetting opportunity cost of investment in A/R

**Carrying Cost:**
= Average A/R × Cost of capital
= (Sales/365 × Collection period) × Cost %

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit risk: Customer may not pay, creating bad debt
5 Cs: Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions
Credit extends sales but increases bad debts and carrying costs
Extend credit if: CM > Expected bad debt loss
Credit terms: Discount/discount period, net/net period
Policy changes: Analyze incremental profit impact
Include carrying cost (investment in A/R × cost of capital)

knowledgeCheck.question: A product has $100 selling price, $70 variable cost, and estimated 5% bad debt rate. What is the expected profit from a credit sale?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: CM = $100 - $70 = $30. Expected profit = 0.95 × $30 - 0.05 × $70 = $28.50 - $3.50 = $25.00... Wait, let me recalculate: 95% pay: we get $30 CM. 5% don't pay: we lose $70 variable cost. Expected = 0.95($30) - 0.05($70) = $28.50 - $3.50 = $25.00.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider both the CM from payers and loss from non-payers.

title: Enterprise Risk Management Framework
description: Apply COSO ERM to organizational risk management

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Risk is everywhere in business! CMAs support enterprise-wide risk management by identifying, measuring, and monitoring risks. Understanding ERM frameworks helps integrate risk thinking into strategic planning and operational decisions.

sections.title: ERM Overview
sections.content:
**Enterprise Risk Management (ERM):**
• Holistic view of organizational risks
• Integrated into strategy and performance
• Board and management responsibility
• Not just risk avoidance - risk optimization

**ERM vs Traditional Risk Management:**
• Traditional: Siloed, department-level
• ERM: Enterprise-wide, integrated
• Traditional: Defensive
• ERM: Strategic, value-creating

**Key Frameworks:**
• COSO ERM (most common)
• ISO 31000

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Key ERM Concepts
sections.content:
**Risk Appetite:**
• Amount of risk willing to accept
• Set by board
• Aligned with strategy
• Qualitative or quantitative

**Risk Tolerance:**
• Acceptable variation around objectives
• More specific than appetite
• Often quantified

**Risk Capacity:**
• Maximum risk organization can absorb
• Before threatening viability
• Financial and operational limits

**Relationship:**
Capacity > Appetite > Tolerance

Capacity = The most we CAN take
Appetite = What we WANT to take
Tolerance = Acceptable range

sections.title: Risk Identification and Assessment
sections.content:
**Risk Identification Methods:**
• Brainstorming sessions
• Historical analysis
• Industry benchmarking
• Process analysis
• SWOT analysis
• Scenario planning

**Risk Assessment:**
• **Likelihood:** Probability of occurrence
• **Impact:** Severity if it occurs
• **Velocity:** Speed of impact

**Risk Map (Heat Map):**
• Plot risks by likelihood and impact
• Prioritize high/high quadrant
• Visual communication tool

**Inherent vs Residual Risk:**
• Inherent: Before controls
• Residual: After controls applied

sections.title: Risk Responses
sections.content:
**Four Risk Response Options:**

**Avoid:**
• Eliminate the activity
• Exit the market
• Don't pursue opportunity

**Reduce (Mitigate):**
• Implement controls
• Lower likelihood or impact
• Most common response

**Share (Transfer):**
• Insurance
• Partnerships
• Contracts (indemnification)
• Hedging

**Accept:**
• Within risk appetite
• Cost of response > benefit
• Document and monitor

**Choice depends on:** Risk vs reward, cost of response, risk appetite

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ERM vs Internal Control
sections.content:
**Don't Confuse:**

**COSO ERM:**
• Risk-focused
• Strategic and operational
• 5 components (2017 update)
• Broader scope
• Upside and downside risk

**COSO Internal Control:**
• Control-focused
• Financial reporting focus
• 5 components (different ones)
• Narrower scope
• Primarily downside risk

**Relationship:**
• Internal control is PART OF ERM
• ERM is broader strategic framework
• Both from COSO

**Exam Tip:**
Know which framework is being referenced

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ERM: Enterprise-wide, integrated risk management
COSO ERM: 5 components including governance and strategy
Risk appetite: Amount of risk willing to accept
Risk tolerance: Acceptable variation around objectives
Assess risks by likelihood, impact, and velocity
Responses: Avoid, Reduce, Share, Accept
Internal control is part of, but narrower than, ERM

knowledgeCheck.question: The amount of risk an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives is called:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Risk appetite is the amount of risk an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of value. It's a strategic concept set by the board and guides risk-taking throughout the organization. Risk tolerance is the acceptable variation around specific objectives.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which term describes willingness to accept risk?

title: Operational Risk Management
description: Identify and mitigate risks in business operations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Operations can fail in many ways! CMAs help identify operational risks, quantify their impact, and develop mitigation strategies. Understanding operational risk is crucial for protecting value and ensuring business continuity.

sections.title: Operational Risk Defined
sections.content:
**Operational Risk:**
Risk of loss from inadequate or failed:
• Internal processes
• People
• Systems
• External events

**Examples:**
• System failure or cyber attack
• Employee fraud or error
• Supply chain disruption
• Natural disasters
• Regulatory non-compliance
• Product defects
• Process breakdowns

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk Assessment Matrix
sections.content:
**Risk Assessment:**

**Likelihood × Impact = Risk Priority**

| | Low Impact | High Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **High Likelihood** | Monitor | Top Priority |
| **Low Likelihood** | Accept | Prepare |

**Focus on:**
• High likelihood + high impact = Immediate action
• Low likelihood + high impact = Contingency plans
• High likelihood + low impact = Controls and monitoring
• Low likelihood + low impact = Accept or ignore

**Memory:**
'Likely and Lethal = Lead with action'

sections.title: Business Continuity Planning
sections.content:
**Business Continuity Plan (BCP):**
• How to continue operations during disruption
• Identifies critical business functions
• Recovery strategies and timelines

**Key Elements:**
• **Business Impact Analysis (BIA):** Which processes are critical?
• **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** How quickly must we recover?
• **Recovery Point Objective (RPO):** How much data loss is acceptable?

**Disaster Recovery (DR):**
• Subset of BCP
• Focus on IT systems recovery
• Backup sites, data restoration

**Testing:**
• Tabletop exercises
• Walkthrough drills
• Full simulation tests

sections.title: Key Risk Indicators (KRIs)
sections.content:
**Key Risk Indicators:**
Metrics that signal increasing risk

**Examples:**
• System downtime frequency
• Employee turnover rate
• Customer complaints
• Failed quality inspections
• Cybersecurity incidents
• Near-miss events

**Characteristics of Good KRIs:**
• Leading (not lagging)
• Measurable and objective
• Relevant to key risks
• Have defined thresholds
• Trigger action when exceeded

**KRI vs KPI:**
• KRI: Risk focused (what could go wrong)
• KPI: Performance focused (how are we doing)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Risk vs Control
sections.content:
**Don't Confuse:**

**Risk:**
• What could go wrong
• Potential negative event
• Uncertainty about outcomes
• Examples: Fraud, system failure

**Control:**
• What we do about it
• Action to mitigate risk
• Preventive or detective
• Examples: Segregation of duties, backups

**Inherent Risk:**
• Risk before controls
• Raw exposure

**Residual Risk:**
• Risk after controls
• What remains despite mitigation

**Formula:**
Inherent Risk - Control Effectiveness = Residual Risk

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Operational risk: Losses from failed processes, people, systems, or external events
Categories: People, Process, Systems, External
Assess by likelihood × impact
BCP: Plan for business continuation during disruption
RTO: Recovery time; RPO: Acceptable data loss
KRIs: Leading indicators of increasing risk
Residual risk = Inherent risk after controls

knowledgeCheck.question: A company determines that its order processing system must be restored within 4 hours of failure to avoid significant customer impact. This 4-hour target is the:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) is the maximum acceptable time to restore a system or process after disruption. RPO relates to data loss tolerance (how far back in time can data be lost). The 4-hour restoration target is an RTO.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which term refers to how quickly a system must be restored?
